initial_prompt: Objective: Exploit the 'Book' machine (Linux, IP: 10.129.1.55) to gain initial access as the 'reader' user and escalate privileges to 'root'. Attacker Machine IP: 10.10.14.64. Key vulnerabilities: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) for initial access, log poisoning (logrotten) for privilege escalation.

steps.thought: Identify open ports and services on the target machine (10.129.1.55). This is the first step in reconnaissance.
steps.result: Nmap scan completed. Open ports found: 22/tcp (SSH OpenSSH 7.6p1) and 80/tcp (HTTP Apache httpd 2.4.6). This suggests a web application on port 80 as a potential entry point.

steps.thought: Investigate the web service on port 80 to understand its content and functionality.
steps.result: Successfully fetched the homepage. The title suggests a book library application. A login page is visible.

steps.thought: Enumerate directories and files on the web server to find hidden pages or functionalities.
steps.result: Dirb scan completed. Standard directories like `/login`, `/admin` (possibly restricted), and `/uploads` might be found. Confirms a functional web application. No critical hidden directories immediately obvious beyond standard ones.

steps.thought: Create an account on the web application to explore authenticated features. This often reveals more attack surface.
steps.command: Manual Action: Navigate to http://10.129.1.55 in a browser and complete the sign-up process (e.g., with username 'Raj').
steps.result: User account 'Raj' successfully created. After logging in, a dashboard is accessible with features like book collection viewing, book uploading ('Collection' tab), and a 'Contact Us' page. The email 'admin@book.htb' is noted on the site.

steps.thought: Add the discovered domain 'book.htb' to the attacker's /etc/hosts file, mapping it to the target IP. This can be useful for interacting with the web application if it uses host-based routing or for SSL certificates.
steps.result: The entry '10.129.1.55 book.htb' has been added to /etc/hosts on the attacker machine.

steps.thought: Craft a payload to test for Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) by attempting to read '/etc/passwd'. This payload will be embedded in a file to be uploaded.
steps.result: File 'ssrf_passwd_payload.html' created locally, containing the JavaScript payload for SSRF to read '/etc/passwd'.

steps.thought: Upload the crafted SSRF payload ('ssrf_passwd_payload.html') through the web application's file upload feature (e.g., 'Collection' tab) and trigger the vulnerable PDF generation/export function.
steps.command: Manual Action: Upload 'ssrf_passwd_payload.html' via the web application and trigger the PDF export/preview feature.
steps.result: The payload was processed by the application. The resulting PDF (or application output) contains the content of '/etc/passwd', confirming the SSRF vulnerability.

steps.thought: Craft another SSRF payload, this time to extract the SSH private key of the 'reader' user, located at '/home/reader/.ssh/id_rsa'.
steps.result: File 'ssrf_reader_key_payload.html' created locally, containing the JavaScript payload for SSRF to read 'reader' user's SSH private key.

steps.thought: Upload the new SSRF payload ('ssrf_reader_key_payload.html') to extract the 'reader' user's SSH private key using the same vulnerable PDF generation/export function.
steps.command: Manual Action: Upload 'ssrf_reader_key_payload.html' via the web application and trigger the PDF export/preview feature.
steps.result: The payload was processed. The resulting PDF (or application output) contains the SSH private key for the 'reader' user.

steps.thought: Save the extracted SSH private key for the 'reader' user into a local file on the attacker machine.
steps.command: echo "-----BEGIN RSA PRIVATE KEY-----\n[...content of reader's private key...]\n-----END RSA PRIVATE KEY-----" > reader_id_rsa
steps.result: The 'reader' user's SSH private key has been saved to the file 'reader_id_rsa'.

steps.thought: Set appropriate permissions (read/write for owner only) for the saved SSH private key file to allow its use with the SSH client.
steps.result: Permissions for 'reader_id_rsa' set to 600.

steps.thought: Use the extracted SSH private key to log in to the target machine as the 'reader' user.
steps.result: Successfully logged into 10.129.1.55 as user 'reader'. Initial access gained.

steps.thought: Retrieve the user flag, typically located in the user's home directory.
steps.result: User flag displayed (e.g., d4e8e8f21e1602c7a1588ddf730d5f8c). First objective achieved.

steps.thought: On the attacker machine, create a directory to store tools that will be served via HTTP for transfer to the target.
steps.result: Directory '~/book_ctf_share' created on the attacker machine.

steps.thought: Download pspy64 (a process snooping tool) to the shared directory on the attacker machine.
steps.result: pspy64 downloaded to '~/book_ctf_share/pspy64'.

steps.thought: Navigate to the shared directory on the attacker machine.
steps.result: Current directory changed to '~/book_ctf_share'.

steps.thought: Clone the 'logrotten' exploit repository from GitHub into the shared directory. This tool exploits log rotation vulnerabilities.
steps.result: Logrotten repository cloned into '~/book_ctf_share/logrotten'.

steps.thought: Navigate into the cloned 'logrotten' directory to compile the exploit.
steps.result: Current directory changed to '~/book_ctf_share/logrotten'.

steps.thought: Compile the 'logrotten.c' source file to create an executable.
steps.result: 'logrotten.c' compiled successfully. Executable 'logrotten' created in the current directory ('~/book_ctf_share/logrotten/').

steps.thought: Copy the compiled 'logrotten' executable to the parent shared directory ('~/book_ctf_share') so it can be served by the HTTP server.
steps.result: Compiled 'logrotten' executable copied to '~/book_ctf_share/'.

steps.thought: Navigate back to the main shared directory ('~/book_ctf_share') to start the HTTP server.
steps.result: Current directory changed to '~/book_ctf_share'.

steps.thought: Start a simple HTTP server on the attacker machine (port 8000) to serve 'pspy64' and 'logrotten' to the target machine. This command will run in the foreground.
steps.result: HTTP server started on 0.0.0.0 port 8000, serving files from '~/book_ctf_share'. (Leave this running in a separate terminal).

steps.thought: On the target machine (as 'reader'), download 'pspy64' from the attacker's HTTP server to the '/tmp' directory.
steps.result: 'pspy64' downloaded from attacker to '/tmp/pspy64' on the target machine.

steps.thought: Make 'pspy64' executable on the target machine.
steps.result: Execute permissions set for '/tmp/pspy64'.

steps.thought: Run 'pspy64' on the target machine to monitor processes and identify any cron jobs or recurring tasks, especially those run by root.
steps.result: Process monitoring started. Output shows '/usr/sbin/logrotate /etc/logrotate.conf' running as root periodically (e.g., every minute). This is a potential target for log poisoning.

steps.thought: On the target machine (as 'reader'), download the compiled 'logrotten' exploit from the attacker's HTTP server to the '/tmp' directory.
steps.result: 'logrotten' exploit downloaded from attacker to '/tmp/logrotten' on the target machine.

steps.thought: Make the 'logrotten' exploit executable on the target machine.
steps.result: Execute permissions set for '/tmp/logrotten'.

steps.thought: Create the payload script ('ignite') on the target machine in '/tmp'. This script will be executed by root via the logrotten exploit to copy root's SSH key.
steps.result: Payload script '/tmp/ignite' created. It will copy root's SSH key to '/tmp/root_ssh_key' and make it readable.

steps.thought: Make the 'ignite' payload script executable.
steps.result: Execute permissions set for '/tmp/ignite'.

steps.thought: Execute 'logrotten' on the target machine, pointing it to the 'ignite' payload and a log file that 'reader' can write to and is rotated by root (e.g., '/home/reader/backups/access.log').
steps.result: 'logrotten' executed. It will attempt to poison '/home/reader/backups/access.log'. When logrotate runs as root, the '/tmp/ignite' payload should execute. Root's SSH key is expected at '/tmp/root_ssh_key'.

steps.thought: After waiting for logrotate to trigger (usually within a minute or two), check if the root's SSH private key has been copied to '/tmp/root_ssh_key'.
steps.result: File '/tmp/root_ssh_key' exists, indicating the logrotten exploit was successful and the root SSH key has been exfiltrated.

steps.thought: Display the content of the exfiltrated root SSH key to verify.
steps.result: The content of root's SSH private key is displayed on the terminal.

steps.thought: Copy the root's SSH private key from '/tmp' to the 'reader' user's home directory for easier management.
steps.result: Root's SSH key copied to '/home/reader/root_final.key'.

steps.thought: Set appropriate permissions (read/write for owner only) for the copied root SSH key file.
steps.result: Permissions for '/home/reader/root_final.key' set to 600.

steps.thought: Use the stolen root SSH private key to log in as 'root' on the target machine.
steps.result: Successfully logged into 10.129.1.55 as user 'root'. Privilege escalation complete.

steps.thought: Navigate to the root user's home directory.
steps.result: Current directory changed to '/root'.

steps.thought: Retrieve the root flag to complete the challenge.
steps.result: Root flag displayed (e.g., f2c9b8e0cc3c9a6d46d39f3a8b7b1e0a). Challenge completed.
